• The analysis aim is set.
• An eﬃciency measure is chosen
Example: elementary operation counting, running time, etc.
• Input data characteristics are set.
• The algorithm is implemented.
• Input data are generated.
• The program is run for for all input data; the result are stored.
• The results are analyzed.

• A function f() calls directly a function g() if the deﬁnition of f()
contains at least one call to g().
• A function f() calls indirectly a function g() if f() calls directly a
function h(), and h() calls directly or indirectly the function g().
• A function f() is recursively deﬁned if it calls itself directly or
indirectly.
A recursive function deﬁnition:

• Base case testing – recursive calls stopping condition.
• Recursive (general case): some variable (integer) is passed as
parameter to the function itself such that after a ﬁnite number of
calls to reach the base case.
Remark: There exists recursive function with no parameters.
Example 1. Factorial function deﬁnition:

• Base case: 0! = 1;
• General case: n! = n × ((n − 1)!), n > 0.
Function factorial(n)
begin
if n <= 1 then

return 1
else
return (n ∗ factorial(n − 1))
end

Function factorial(n)
begin
product ← 1
while n > 1 do
product ← product ∗ n
n ← n − 1
return product
end
Example 2. Fibonacci numbers:

• f (0) = 0, f (1) = 1,
• f (n) = f (n − 1) + f (n − 2), n > 1.
Function ﬁb(n)
begin
if n <= 1 then

return n
else
return ﬁb(n − 1) + ﬁb(n − 2)
end

Function iﬁb(n)
begin
f 0 ← 0
f 1 ← 1
if n <= 1 then

return n
else
for k ← 2 to n do
temp ← f 1
f 1 ← f 1 + f 0
f 0 ← temp
return f 1
end

• To estimate the running time:
• a recurrence is set in order to express the relation between the
execution time of the initial problem and the execution time of the
reduced problem;
• the recurrence is solved.
• Example: for the factorial, the recurrence that estimates the running
time is:
T (n) =
(cid:26) 0,
n = 0
T (n − 1) + 1, n > 1

1. Substitution method. A limit is guessed and is proved by induction.
2. Iteration method. The recurrence is iterated and expressed as a sum
of terms that depends only on the problem size and the initial
condition (base cases).
3. Recurrence tree. Expresses the recurrence as a tree (nodes
corresponds to costs).
4. Master theorem. Provides limits for recurrences of type
T (n) = aT (n/b) + f (n)

• The solution is guessed.
• Mathematical induction is employed to determine the constants and
to proof that the solution is correct.
Find an upper bound for the recurrence T (n) = 2T ((cid:98)n/2(cid:99)) + n

• Guessed solution: T (n) = O(n log n).
• To be proved by induction: T (n) ≤ cn log n, for c > 0.
Suppose that the relation is true for all positive values m < n, in
particular for m = (cid:98)n/2(cid:99): T ((cid:98)n/2(cid:99)) ≤ c(cid:98)n/2(cid:99) log((cid:98)n/2(cid:99)).
T (n) ≤ 2(c(cid:98)n/2(cid:99) log((cid:98)n/2(cid:99))) + n
≤ cn log((cid:98)n/2(cid:99)) + n

= cn log n − cn log 2 + n
= cn log n − cn + n
≤ cn log n, for c ≥ 1
It has to be shown that the relation holds for the base case (limit

conditions). T (1) = 1 ≤ c1 log 1 = 0
Base cases: T(2) and T(3) ( n0 = 2 )
T (2) = 4 and T (3) = 5, T (2) ≤ c2 log 2 and

• Subtraction of an inferior term order (to consolidate the inductive
hypothesis).
Example: T (n) = T ((cid:98)n/2(cid:99)) + T ((cid:100)n/2(cid:101)) + 1
• Guessed solution: T (n) = O(n).
• It is shown by induction that T (n) ≤ cn, for c > 0.
T (n) ≤ c(cid:98)n/2(cid:99) + c(cid:100)n/2(cid:101) + 1
= cn + 1
• It is shown by induction that T (n) ≤ cn − d, d >= 0 const.
T (n) ≤ (c(cid:98)n/2(cid:99) − d) + (c(cid:100)n/2(cid:101) − d) + 1
= cn − 2d + 1
≤ cn − d, for d ≥ 1
The c constant has to be chosen such that the limit conditions are
satisﬁed.

• Traps
Example: T (n) = 2T ((cid:98)n/2(cid:99)) + n
It is shown (“false”) that T (n) = O(n) guessing that T (n) ≤ cn and:
T (n) ≤ 2(c(cid:98)n/2(cid:99)) + n
≤ cn + n
= O(n), ⇐ false!!
Error: it was not proven the exact form of the induction hypothesis.

• Variable changes.
Example: T (n) = 2T ((cid:98)sqrt(n)(cid:99)) + log n
The recurrence is simpliﬁed by variable change: m = log n.
T (2m) = 2T (2m/2) + m
Rename S(m) = T (2m), and it follows S(m) = 2S(m/2) + m.
S(m) = O(m log m),
T (n) = T (2m) = S(m) = O(m log m) = O(log n log log n).

Substitution method: implies to guess the solution (!)

• Recurrence trees:
• allows to visualize a recurrence;
• each node corresponds to the cost of a sub-problem;
• the costs are sum on levels and then they are summed to get the total
cost.
• The recurrence tree can be used to generate a value for the
substitution method.
• The recurrence tree is created for T (n) = 3T (n/4) + cn2, c > 0

• The dimension of a sub-problem corresponding to a node on level i:
n/4i ⇒ sub-problem size becomes n = 1 when
n/4i = 1 ⇔ i = log4n ⇒ the tree has log4n + 1 levels.
• The number of nodes on level i: 3i .
• The cost of any node on level i : c(n/4i )2.
• The total cost of nodes on level i: 3i c(n/4i )2 = (3/16)i cn2
(last level log4n: nlog43T (1).)
T (n) = cn2 +

• The substitution method is employed to verify that T (n) = O(n2) is
an upper bound for the relation T (n) = 3T ((cid:98)n/4(cid:99)) + Θ(n2).
• it is shown that T (n) ≤ dn2, for d > 0
T (n) ≤ 3T ((cid:98)n/4(cid:99)) + cn2
≤ 3d(cid:98)n/4(cid:99)2 + cn2
≤ 3d(n/4)2 + c(n2)

≤ dn2, for d ≥ (16/13)c
• Provides a method to solve recurrences of type

T (n) = aT (n/b) + f (n) where a ≥ 1 and b > 1 are constants, and
f (n) is a function asymptotically positive.

• Master theorem:
Let a ≥ 1 and b > 1 constants, f (n) a function and T (n) deﬁned on
non-negative integer numbers by the recurrence relation:

T (n) = aT (n/b) + f (n). Then:
1. If f (n) = O(nlogba−(cid:15)) for (cid:15) > 0 constant, then T (n) = Θ(nlogba).
2. If f (n) = Θ(nlogba), then T (n) = Θ(nlogba log n).
3. If f (n) = Ω(nlogba+(cid:15)) for (cid:15) > 0 constant, and if af (n/b) ≤ cf (n) for
c < 1 and n large enough, then T (n) = Θ(f (n)).
a = 9, b = 3, f (n) = n and nlogba = nlog39 = Θ(n2).

Since f (n) = O(nlog39−(cid:15)), with (cid:15) = 1, the case 1 of Master theorem
can be applied ⇒ T (n) = Θ(n2).
a = 1, b = 3/2, f (n) = 1 and nlogba = nlog3/21 = n0 = 1.

Since f (n) = Θ(nlogba) = Θ(1), the case 2 of Master theorem can be
applied ⇒ T (n) = Θ(log n).
• T (n) = 3T (n/4) + n log n
a = 3, b = 4, f (n) = n log n and nlogba = nlog43 = O(n0.793)

Since f (n) = Ω(nlog43+(cid:15)), with (cid:15) ≈ 0.2, the case 3 of Master theorem
can be applied if: af (n/b) = 3(n/4) log(n/4) ≤ (3/4)n log n = cf (n)
for c = 3/4 and n large enough. It follows T (n) = Θ(n log n).
• The Master theorem cannot be applied for T (n) = 2T (n/2) + n log n
a = 2, b = 2, f (n) = n log n and nlogba = n

Since f (n) = n log n is asymptotically larger the nlogba = n, it follows
hat the case 3 can be applied (false!!).
f (n) is not polynomial larger.

f (n)/nlogba = (n log n)/n = log n is asymptotically smaller than n(cid:15),
for any positive constant (cid:15).
